title: Witch Week - Chapter 3 (Fragment 3)
text:
Nobody behaved suspiciously. Most people’s idea was to get the acting over as quickly as possible. Some argued a little, for the look of the thing. Others tried running about to make things seem dramatic. And first prize for brevity certainly went to Simon Silverson and Karen Grigg. Simon said, “I know you’re a witch, so don’t argue.”

And Karen replied, “Yes, I am. I give in. Let’s stop now.”

By the time it came to Nirupam, Miss Hodge’s list of suspects was all bottom and no top. Then Nirupam put on a terrifying performance as inquisitor. His eyes blazed. His voice alternately roared and fell to a sinister whisper. He pointed fiercely at Estelle’s face. “Look at your evil eyes!” he bellowed. Then he whispered, “I see you, I feel you, I know you—you are a witch!” Estelle was so frightened that she gave a real performance of terrified innocence. But Brian Wentworth’s performance as a witch outshone even Nirupam. Brian wept, he cringed, he made obviously false excuses, and he ended kneeling at Delia Martin’s feet, sobbing for mercy and crying real tears.

Everyone was astonished, including Miss Hodge. She would dearly have liked to put Brian at the top of her list of suspects, as either the witch or the one who wrote the note. But how bothersome for her plans if she had to go to Mr. Wentworth and say it was Brian. No, she decided. There was no genuine feeling in Brian’s performance, and the same went for Nirupam. They were both just good actors.

Then it was the turn of Charles and Nan. Charles had seen it coming for some time now, that he would be paired with Nan. He was very annoyed. He seemed to be haunted by her today. But he did not intend to let that stop his performance from being a triumph of comic acting. He was depressed by the lack of invention everyone except Nirupam had shown. Nobody had thought of making the inquisitor funny. “I’ll be inquisitor,” he said quickly.

But Nan was still smarting from the broomstick. She thought Charles was getting at her and glared at him. Charles, on principle, never let anyone glare at him without giving his nastiest double-barreled stare in return. So they shuffled to the front of the class looking daggers at one another.

There Charles beat at his forehead. “Emergency!” he exclaimed. “There are no witches for the autumn bonfires. I shall have to find an ordinary person instead.” He pointed at Nan. “You’ll do,” he said. “Starting from now, you’re a witch.”

Nan had not realized that the acting had begun. Besides, she was too hurt and angry to care. “Oh, no, I’m not!” she snapped. “Why shouldn’t you be the witch?”

“Because I can prove you’re a witch,” Charles said, trying to stick to his part. “Being an inquisitor, I can prove anything.”

“In that case,” said Nan, angrily ignoring the fine acting, “we’ll both be inquisitors, and I’ll prove you’re a witch too! Why not? You have four of the most evil eyes I ever saw. And your feet smell.”

All eyes turned to Charles’s feet. Since he had been forced to run around the field in the shoes he was wearing now, they were still rather wet. And, being warmed through, they were indeed exuding a small but definite smell.

vocabulary.definition: the art or occupation of performing in plays, movies, or television
vocabulary.example: Most people’s idea was to get the acting over as quickly as possible.

vocabulary.definition: to give reasons for or against something; to dispute
vocabulary.example: Some argued a little, for the look of the thing.

vocabulary.definition: very surprised
vocabulary.example: Everyone was astonished, including Miss Hodge.

vocabulary.definition: to act in a particular way, especially in a good or acceptable way
vocabulary.example: Nobody behaved suspiciously.

vocabulary.definition: to shout something with a deep loud voice
vocabulary.example: ” he bellowed.

vocabulary.definition: the quality of being brief or terse
vocabulary.example: And first prize for brevity certainly went to Simon Silverson and Karen Grigg.

vocabulary.definition: the long handle of a broom
vocabulary.example: But Nan was still smarting from the broomstick.

vocabulary.definition: funny; causing laughter
vocabulary.example: But he did not intend to let that stop his performance from being a triumph of comic acting.

vocabulary.definition: very much
vocabulary.example: She would dearly have liked to put Brian at the top of her list of suspects, as either the witch or the one who wrote the note.

vocabulary.definition: in a state of unhappiness or despondency
vocabulary.example: He was depressed by the lack of invention everyone except Nirupam had shown.

vocabulary.definition: having two barrels or parts; in this context, an expression meaning double or twofold in intensity
vocabulary.example: Charles, on principle, never let anyone glare at him without giving his nastiest double-barreled stare in return.

vocabulary.definition: sudden and impressive; exciting or exaggerated
vocabulary.example: Others tried running about to make things seem dramatic.

vocabulary.definition: a serious, unexpected situation requiring immediate action
vocabulary.example: “Emergency!

vocabulary.definition: to cry out suddenly in surprise, anger, or pain
vocabulary.example: ” he exclaimed.

vocabulary.definition: to discharge gradually or to ooze out
vocabulary.example: And, being warmed through, they were indeed exuding a small but definite smell.

vocabulary.definition: feeling fear or worry
vocabulary.example: ” Estelle was so frightened that she gave a real performance of terrified innocence.

vocabulary.definition: to look at someone angrily
vocabulary.example: She thought Charles was getting at her and glared at him.

vocabulary.definition: the state of being not guilty of a crime or offense
vocabulary.example: ” Estelle was so frightened that she gave a real performance of terrified innocence.

vocabulary.definition: a person who asks questions in a hostile or official manner, historically known for intense investigation or interrogation
vocabulary.example: Then Nirupam put on a terrifying performance as inquisitor.

vocabulary.definition: the act of creating or designing something that has not existed before
vocabulary.example: He was depressed by the lack of invention everyone except Nirupam had shown.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that is easily noticed or understood
vocabulary.example: Brian wept, he cringed, he made obviously false excuses, and he ended kneeling at Delia Martin’s feet, sobbing for mercy and crying real tears.

vocabulary.definition: to be much better than someone else at doing something
vocabulary.example: But Brian Wentworth’s performance as a witch outshone even Nirupam.

vocabulary.definition: an act of presenting a play, concert, or other form of entertainment
vocabulary.example: Then Nirupam put on a terrifying performance as inquisitor.

vocabulary.definition: a fundamental truth or law as the basis of reasoning or conduct
vocabulary.example: Charles, on principle, never let anyone glare at him without giving his nastiest double-barreled stare in return.

vocabulary.definition: to walk by dragging one's feet along or without lifting them fully from the ground
vocabulary.example: So they shuffled to the front of the class looking daggers at one another.

vocabulary.definition: suggesting or threatening evil or harm
vocabulary.example: His voice alternately roared and fell to a sinister whisper.

vocabulary.definition: to feel a sharp, stinging pain
vocabulary.example: But Nan was still smarting from the broomstick.

vocabulary.definition: in a way that makes you feel that something is wrong or that someone is behaving wrongly
vocabulary.example: Nobody behaved suspiciously.

vocabulary.definition: causing great fear
vocabulary.example: Then Nirupam put on a terrifying performance as inquisitor.
